plus_one_r'.solution:
Theorem plus_one_r' : forall n:nat,
  n + 1 = S n.
Proof.
  apply nat_ind.
  - reflexivity.
  - intros n' IHn'. simpl. rewrite IHn'. reflexivity.
Qed.
plus_one_r'.explanation: Use nat_ind directly instead of the induction tactic. Base case is trivial; inductive step uses simpl and the IH.

booltree_ind.solution:
Definition base_case (P : booltree_property_type) : Prop :=
  P bt_empty.

Definition leaf_case (P : booltree_property_type) : Prop :=
  forall (b : bool), P (bt_leaf b).

Definition branch_case (P : booltree_property_type) : Prop :=
  forall (b : bool) (t1 t2 : booltree), P t1 -> P t2 -> P (bt_branch b t1 t2).

Theorem booltree_ind_type_correct : booltree_ind_type.
Proof. exact booltree_ind. Qed.
booltree_ind.explanation: The induction principle for booltree has three cases: bt_empty, bt_leaf (taking a bool), and bt_branch (taking a bool and two subtrees with IHs).

toy_ind.solution:
Inductive Toy : Type :=
  | con1 (b : bool)
  | con2 (n : nat) (t : Toy).

Theorem Toy_correct : exists f g,
  forall P : Toy -> Prop,
    (forall b : bool, P (f b)) ->
    (forall (n : nat) (t : Toy), P t -> P (g n t)) ->
    forall t : Toy, P t.
Proof. exists con1, con2. exact Toy_ind. Qed.
toy_ind.explanation: The induction principle tells us Toy has two constructors: con1 taking a bool, and con2 taking a nat and a recursive Toy.

tree.solution:
(* tree_ind :
  forall (X : Type) (P : tree X -> Prop),
    (forall x : X, P (leaf x)) ->
    (forall t1 : tree X, P t1 -> forall t2 : tree X, P t2 -> P (node t1 t2)) ->
    forall t : tree X, P t *)
tree.explanation: The induction principle for tree X: P holds for leaf x for all x, and if P holds for t1 and t2 then P holds for node t1 t2.

mytype.solution:
(* Inductive mytype (X : Type) : Type :=
  | constr1 (x : X)
  | constr2 (n : nat)
  | constr3 (m : mytype X) (n : nat). *)
mytype.explanation: Reading off from the induction principle: constr1 takes an X, constr2 takes a nat, constr3 takes a recursive mytype X and a nat.

foo.solution:
(* Inductive foo (X Y : Type) : Type :=
  | bar (x : X)
  | baz (y : Y)
  | quux (f : nat -> foo X Y). *)
foo.explanation: bar takes an X, baz takes a Y, and quux takes a function from nat to foo X Y (with the IH for all nat inputs).

foo'.solution:
(* foo'_ind :
  forall (X : Type) (P : foo' X -> Prop),
    (forall (l : list X) (f : foo' X),
      P f -> P (C1 l f)) ->
    P C2 ->
    forall f : foo' X, P f *)
foo'.explanation: C1 takes a list and a recursive foo', so the IH is on the foo' argument. C2 takes no arguments. Note: list X is not recursive in foo', so no IH for it.

t_tree.solution:
Definition better_t_tree_ind_type : Prop :=
  forall (X : Type) (P : t_tree X -> Prop),
    P t_leaf ->
    (forall (l : t_tree X) (v : X) (r : t_tree X),
      P l -> P r -> P (t_branch (l, v, r))) ->
    forall t : t_tree X, P t.

Definition better_t_tree_ind : better_t_tree_ind_type :=
  fun X P Hleaf Hbranch =>
    fix f (t : t_tree X) : P t :=
      match t with
      | t_leaf => Hleaf
      | t_branch (l, v, r) => Hbranch l v r (f l) (f r)
      end.

Theorem reflect_involution : forall (X : Type) (t : t_tree X),
    reflect (reflect t) = t.
Proof.
  intros X t.
  apply better_t_tree_ind.
  - reflexivity.
  - intros l v r IHl IHr. simpl. rewrite IHl. rewrite IHr. reflexivity.
Qed.
t_tree.explanation: We define a custom induction principle that provides IHs for both subtrees in the tuple, then use it to prove reflect is an involution.

  - unfold P_add_comm. intros m. rewrite add_0_r. reflexivity.

Qed. *)
plus_explicit_prop.explanation: Define explicit propositions P_add_assoc and P_add_comm, then prove them using nat_ind directly.
